events.text:
{
  "decision": "fail",
  "risk": "high",
  "work_stage": "in-service",
  "component_conditions": ["installed-defective", "non-compliant", "degraded"],
  "percent_complete": 100,
  "value": 0,
  "sound_source": "",
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "event_time": 0.0,
  "rate": 0.0,
  "order": [],
  "workable": [],
  "findings": [
    "Schrader cores left in during evacuation restrict flow through service valves, preventing achievement of required deep vacuum levels",
    "System likely operating with elevated moisture and non-condensables due to inadequate evacuation, risking compressor damage and metering device icing",
    "Evacuation procedure non-compliant with manufacturer specs and ACCA standards which require core removal for proper evacuation"
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Recover system refrigerant charge per EPA 608",
    "Remove Schrader cores from service valves",
    "Evacuate system to below 500 microns using proper vacuum pump and micron gauge connected directly to system",
    "Perform vacuum decay test to verify system tightness and dryness",
    "Recharge system with weighed-in refrigerant per manufacturer specs",
    "Verify system operation and document evacuation results"
  ],
  "rationale": "Schrader cores left in during evacuation is a well-known procedural defect that restricts vapor flow through service ports, making it impossible to achieve the deep vacuum (typically below 500 microns) required for proper moisture and non-condensable removal. With the system now in service, trapped moisture and non-condensables cause elevated head pressure, reduced efficiency, compressor overheating, and potential premature failure. This is a non-compliant installation defect that compromises system reliability and longevity.",
  "references": [
    "mfr specs - evacuation requirements and Schrader core removal procedures",
    "ACCA Standard 4/5 - installation and evacuation best practices",
    "EPA 608 - refrigerant recovery and handling requirements for corrective work"
  ]
}
